title: Perišić Handball
summary: Ivan Perišić's arm makes contact with the ball in the penalty area during the 2018 World Cup Final.
lawInvolved: Law 12 (Fouls and Misconduct - Handling the ball)
evidenceShown: Video/images of the ball striking the arm; player's running posture.

perspectives.text: It's a clear penalty. His arm blocked the cross and gave an unfair advantage.
The arm was in a natural position for a player jumping. There was no intent to handle the ball.
The arm position was unnaturally bigger, creating a barrier. This is a clear and obvious error by the referee.
It is an offense if a player deliberately touches the ball or makes their body unnaturally bigger.

reveal: The word "deliberately" appears four times in Law 12 and has no definition. "Unnaturally bigger" has no definition.
decision2Prompt: Now that you know the law doesn't define these terms, is it still a clear penalty?
reflection: The certainty of the first decision was built on undefined terms. Mental state is unobservable.
emotionalObjective: Shock and realization that the rulebook is hollow at its core.

title: VAR Nested Layer
summary: The VAR intervention mechanism regarding the Perišić handball.
evidenceShown: The referee's initial "no penalty" call vs. the VAR recommendation to review and overturn.

perspectives.text: The initial call was completely wrong; VAR saved the game.
It was a subjective, 50/50 call. It wasn't obviously wrong enough to warrant an overturn.
The missed handball was a clear error that directly impacted a goal-scoring opportunity.
VAR may only intervene in the event of a "clear and obvious error" or "serious missed incident."

reveal: "Clear and obvious" has no definition. The correction mechanism itself relies on an interpretive judgment.
decision2Prompt: Does a review mechanism based on an undefined term solve the ambiguity, or just move it?
reflection: We replaced an undefined foul with an undefined standard for correction.
emotionalObjective: Epiphany about systemic ambiguity in officiating technology.

title: De Jong Chest Kick
summary: Nigel de Jong kicks Xabi Alonso in the chest during the 2010 World Cup Final.
evidenceShown: The high boot to the chest. Referee Howard Webb issuing a yellow card, and his later quote stating it should have been red.

perspectives.text: It's assault. A clear red card in any league in the world.
In the context of a World Cup Final, it looked reckless but lacked the malicious intent required to ruin the game with a red card.
The player used excessive force and endangered the safety of an opponent. It is serious foul play.
Reckless challenges warrant a yellow card. Using excessive force constitutes serious foul play (red card).

reveal: Howard Webb gave a yellow, but years later said it should have been red. Same referee, same footage, two answers. The boundary between "reckless" and "excessive force" is undefined.
decision2Prompt: If the same referee gives two different answers to the exact same footage, what is the "correct" call?
reflection: The boundary between a caution and a sending-off is fundamentally undefined.
emotionalObjective: Resignation to the impossibility of objective consistency.

title: Mbappé "Deliberate Play" Offside
summary: Kylian Mbappé receives the ball in an offside position, but a defender's touch resets the play, allowing him to score in the 2021 Nations League Final.
evidenceShown: Mbappé's position, the pass, the defender's attempt to intercept, and the resulting goal.

perspectives.text: He was miles offside when the ball was kicked! The goal shouldn't stand.
The defender intentionally moved to intercept the ball. This constitutes a deliberate play, resetting the offside phase.
The defender's action meets the criteria for a deliberate play. The offside offense is nullified.
A player in an offside position receiving the ball from an opponent who deliberately played the ball is not considered to have gained an advantage.

reveal: "Deliberately played" has no definition. Its guidance terms (e.g., "in control") are also undefined. It is undefined all the way down.
decision2Prompt: Can you enforce an offside rule if the condition that resets it is undefined?
reflection: The law defines its very area of application using undefined terms.

title: Suárez Goal-Line Handball
summary: Luis Suárez intentionally handles the ball on the goal line to prevent a certain goal for Ghana in the 2010 World Cup Quarter-Final.
disputedTerm: Adequacy of punishment / Procedural vs. Substantive Justice
evidenceShown: The deliberate handball, the red card, the missed penalty, and Uruguay advancing.

perspectives.text: He cheated, broke the rules intentionally, and his team was rewarded with a trip to the semi-finals.
I applied the law perfectly. I sent the player off and awarded a penalty kick. That is the maximum punishment allowed.
The referee correctly identified the denial of an obvious goal-scoring opportunity by handball. Red card and penalty are the correct sanctions.
Denying the opposing team a goal or an obvious goal-scoring opportunity by a handball offense results in a sending-off and a penalty kick.

reveal: The law doesn't define 'adequate remedy.' It doesn't define 'justice.' It only defines procedure. Every rule was followed perfectly. Ghana still went home.
reflection: The law is completely silent on whether its remedy fits the context of the situation.
emotionalObjective: A deep sense of unfairness and an understanding of the fundamental limits of the law.
